standard_description: Understand division as an unknown-factor problem. For example, find 32 ÷ 8 by finding the number that makes 32 when multiplied by 8.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Problem 1
32 students shared 6 pizzas equally. How much pizza will each student get?
a.   Find the exact answer.
b.   Check your work.
Problem 2
32 gallons of water is used to completely fill 6 fish tanks. If each tank holds the same amount of water, how many gallons will each tank hold?
a.   Find the exact answer. Write it as a fraction and as a mixed number.
b.   Determine which two whole numbers the answer is in between.
c.   Explain how the fractional part of the mixed number is related to the remainder in the problem.
d.   Check your work.
Problem 3
Solve the following problems. Think about the main differences between each one.
a.   A teacher has 21 batteries. Each calculator uses 4 batteries. How many calculators can the teacher fill with batteries?
b.   Four children can ride in a car. How many cars are needed to take 21 children to the museum?
c.   Ms. Cole bakes 21 cookies and wants to share them out fairly to 4 friends. How many cookies will each friend get?

Problem 1
Solve. Show or explain your work.
a.
$${{1\over3} \div 2}$$
b.
$${{1\over4} \div 9}$$
Problem 2
Zach spends half of his workday tutoring students. If he sees 5 students and spends the same amount of time with each student, what fraction of his workday does he spend tutoring each student?

Problem 1
Nolan gives some Fruit-by-the-Foot to his
$$3$$
friends to share equally.
a.   If he has
$$3$$

$$1$$
foot of Fruit-by-the-Foot, how many feet of Fruit-by-the-Foot will each friend receive?
c.   If he has
$${1\over2}$$
foot of Fruit-by-the-Foot, how many feet of Fruit-by-the-Foot will each friend receive?
d.   If he has
$${1\over3}$$
foot of Fruit-by-the-Foot, how many feet of Fruit-by-the-Foot will each friend receive?

Problem 1
Rudy is waiting patiently for his new hens to lay some eggs.
a.   On Monday he went to the henhouse to check their nests. He has 12 hens in the house, and each hen had laid 0 eggs. How many eggs did Rudy have in total? Write an equation to represent the situation.
b.   On Tuesday when he went to the henhouse, he discovered that each of his 12 hens had laid 1 egg! How many eggs does he have now? Write an equation to represent the situation.

Katia and Gerard are stocking shelves at the grocery store. Katia stocks 3 shelves with 7 boxes of cereal on each shelf. Gerard stocks 7 shelves with 3 boxes of cereal on each shelf.
Katia says they put the same number of cereal boxes on each shelf. Gerard says they didn’t, since they stocked a different number of boxes on each of a different number of shelves. Who do you agree with, Katia or Gerard? Explain.

Problem 1
__________ graham crackers are shared equally by __________ people.
a.   Choose numbers to fill in the blanks. You can only use each number once: 2, 3, 5.
b.   Represent the situation with a diagram or drawing.

items.question_content:
Each square in the figure below has an area of $1$ square unit. We can break the figure into two parts. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the equations to find the area of the entire figure.**  

||
-: | :-
$\text{Area}$ |$=$  [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\redE{\text{ fives}} + \purpleE{4\text{ fives}}$|
|$= \redE{10} + \purpleE{20}$|
|$=$ [[☃ numeric-input 3]]|
hints:
The rectangle has an area of $6\times 5$, or  $6$ fives.

Let's use the figure to break the $6$ fives into two parts.

The striped rectangle breaks up $6$ fives into $\redE{2\text{ fives}}$ and $\purpleE{4\text{ fives}}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The top rectangle has an area of $\redE{2\text{ fives}}$ or $\redE{10}$.

The bottom rectangle has an area of $\purpleE{4\text{ fives}}$ or $\purpleE{20}$.

$\begin{align}
6\times 5&=\redE{2\text{ fives}} + \purpleE{4\text{ fives}}\\\\
&= \redE{10} + \purpleE{20}\\\\
&= 30
\end{align}$

items.question_content:
Let's use the associative property to help us solve $28\times2$.

hints:
To find the product of $\maroonE{28}\times\blueE{2}$, we can break $\maroonE{28}$ into smaller factors.

We will keep the expressions on both sides of the equal sign equivalent.
The expression $7\times4$ equals $28$.

So, $\maroonE{28}$ is the same as $\maroonE{7}$ groups of $\maroonE{4}$.

$\maroonE{28} \times \blueE{2} ={ \maroonE{7}}\times \maroonE{4} \times \blueE{2}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
\maroonE{28} \times \blueE{2} &= \maroonE{7}\times \maroonE{4} \times \blueE{2}\\\\
&= 7 \times (4 \times 2)\\\\
&= 7 \times 8 \\\\
&= 56
\end{align}$

These are the completed equations:

$\begin{align}
\maroonE{28} \times \blueE{2} &=\maroonE{7}\times \maroonE{4} \times \blueE{2}\\\\
\maroonE{28} \times \blueE{2} &={7}\times \purpleE8\\\\
\maroonE{28} \times \blueE{2} &= 56
\end{align}$

items.question_content:
One expression for the area of a $3$ unit by $8$ unit rectangle is $3\times 8$. 

We can also break the figure into two parts.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What are $2$ more expressions that represent the area of the entire rectangle?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The rectangle has an area of $3\times 8$, or  $3$ eights.

Let's use the figure to break the $3$ eights into two parts.

[[☃ image 1]]

The area of the top rectangle is $\blueE{4\text{ threes}}$ or $\blueE{12}$.

The area of the bottom rectangle is 
$\purpleE{4\text{ threes}}$ or $\purpleE{12}$.
We get the total area of the rectangle by adding the areas of the two parts.

The following expressions represent the area of the entire figure:

- $\blueE{4 \text{ threes}} +\purpleE{4\text{ threes}}$
- $\blueE{12} +\purpleE{12}$

items.question_content:
Kaley solved $\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times\maroonE{2}$ using the equations below.

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &=(\redE{10}\times\purpleE{5}) \times\maroonE{2}\\\\ 
&=50 \times\maroonE{2}\\\\\  
&=100
\end{align}$

**Use the equations below to solve $\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2}$ in a different way.**

||
-:|:-
$\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2}$ | $=\redE{10}\times($ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]${}\times\maroonE{2})$  |
|$= \redE{10}\times {}$  [[☃ numeric-input 3]]|
|$= 100$|
hints: The parentheses tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

Kaley solved the equation by multiplying $(\redE{10}\times\purpleE{5})$ first, and then multiplying by $\maroonE{2}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &=(\redE{10}\times\purpleE{5}) \times\maroonE{2}\\\\
&=\blueE{50}\times\maroonE{2}\\\\
&=100
\end{align}$

In the new equation below, we want to multiply $(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{2})$ first, and then multiply by $\redE{10}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &= \redE{10}\times(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{2})\\\\
&=\redE{10}\times\goldE{10}\\\\ 
&=100
\end{align}$

Here are the completed equations:

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &= \redE{10}\times(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{2})\\\\
&=\redE{10}\times\goldE{10}\\\\ 
&=100
\end{align}$

khan_exercises.title: Multiply monomials by polynomials
khan_exercises.description: Use the distributive property to express the product of a monomial and a polynomial as a single polynomial.

items.question_content:
**Expand.**  
*Your answer should be a polynomial in standard form.*

$ 3x(x^2-5x+6)=$ [[☃ expression 2]]
hints:
We expand the parentheses using the **distributive property**:

$\blueD A(B+C+D)=\blueD A\cdot B+\blueD A\cdot C+\blueD A\cdot D$

We can also think about the problem using an area model:

[[☃ image 1]]

Here's how the solution goes, algebraically:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\goldE{3x}(x^2-5x+6)
\\\\
&=\goldE{3x}(x^2)+\goldE{3x}(-5x)+\goldE{3x}(6)
\\\\
&=3x^3-15x^2+18x
\end{align}$

Here's how the solution looks in terms of the area model:

[[☃ image 1]]

In conclusion,

$ 3x(x^2-5x+6)=3x^3-15x^2+18x$
